## UNDERSTANDING LATENT CORRELATION-BASED MULTIVIEW LEARNING AND SELF-SUPERVISION: AN IDENTIFIABILITY PERSPECTIVE
**Qi Lyu Xiao Fu** _[∗]_ **Weiran Wang Songtao Lu** School of EECS School of EECS Google Inc. IBM Research Oregon State Univ. Oregon State Univ. Mountain View Yorktown Heights

## ABSTRACT

Multiple views of data, both naturally acquired (e.g., image and audio) and artificially produced (e.g., via adding different noise to data samples), have proven useful in enhancing representation learning. Natural views are often handled by multiview analysis tools, e.g., (deep) canonical correlation analysis [(D)CCA], while the artificial ones are frequently used in self-supervised learning (SSL) paradigms, e.g., BYOL and Barlow Twins. Both types of approaches often involve learning neural feature extractors such that the embeddings of data exhibit high cross-view correlations. Although intuitive, the effectiveness of correlationbased neural embedding is mostly empirically validated. This work aims to understand latent correlation maximization-based deep multiview learning from a latent component identification viewpoint. An intuitive generative model of multiview data is adopted, where the views are different nonlinear mixtures of shared and private components. Since the shared components are view/distortion-invariant, representing the data using such components is believed to reveal the identity of the samples effectively and robustly. Under this model, latent correlation maximization is shown to guarantee the extraction of the shared components across views (up to certain ambiguities). In addition, it is further shown that the private information in each view can be provably disentangled from the shared using proper regularization design. A finite sample analysis, which has been rare in nonlinear mixture identifiability study, is also presented. The theoretical results and newly designed regularization are tested on a series of tasks.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

One pillar of unsupervised representation learning is multiview learning. Extracting shared information from multiple “views” (e.g., image and audio) of data entities has been considered a major means to fend against noise and data scarcity. A key computational tool for multiview learning is _canonical correlation analysis_ (CCA) (Hotelling, 1936). The classic CCA seeks linear transformation matrices such that transformed views are maximally correlated. A number of works studied nonlinear extensions of CCA; see kernel CCA in (Lai & Fyfe, 2000) and _deep learning-based CCA_ (DCCA) in (Andrew et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2015). DCCA and its variants were shown to largely outperform the classical linear CCA in many tasks.

In recent years, a series of _self-supervised learning_ (SSL) paradigms were proposed. These SSL approaches exhibit a lot of similarities with DCCA approaches, except that the “views” are noisy data “augmenented” from the original clean data. To be specific, different views are generated by distorting data—e.g., using rotating, cropping, and/or adding noise to data samples (Dosovitskiy et al., 2015; Gidaris et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2020; Grill et al., 2020). Then, neural encoders are employed to map these artificial views to embeddings that are highly correlated across views. This genre—which will be referred to as artificial multiview SSL (AM-SSL)—includes some empirically successful frameworks, e.g., BYOL (Grill et al., 2020) and Barlow Twins (Zbontar et al., 2021).

Notably, many DCCA and AM-SSL approaches involve (explicitly or implicitly) searching for highly correlated representations from multiple views, using neural feature extractors (encoders). The empirical success of DCCA and AM-SSL bears an important research question: _How to understand the role of cross-view correlation in deep multiview learning?_ Furthermore, how to use such understanding to design theory-backed learning criteria to serve various purposes?

Intuitively, it makes sense that many DCCA and AM-SSL paradigms involve latent correlation maximization in their loss functions, as such loss functions lead to similar/identical representations from different views—which identifies view-invariant essential information that is often identityrevealing. However, beyond intuition, theoretical support of latent correlation-based deep multiview learning had been less studied, until recent works started exploring this direction in both nonlinear CCA and AM-SSL (see, e.g., (Lyu & Fu, 2020; Von K¨ugelgen et al., 2021; Zimmermann et al., 2021; Tian et al., 2021; Saunshi et al., 2019; Tosh et al., 2021)), but more insights and theoretical underpinnings remain to be discovered under more realistic and challenging settings. In this work, we offer an understanding to the role of latent correlation maximization that is seen in a number of DCCA and AM-SSL systems from a nonlinear mixture learning viewpoint—and use such understanding to assist various learning tasks, e.g., clustering, cross-view translation, and cross-sample generation. Our detailed contributions are:

**(i) Understanding Latent Correlation Maximization - Shared Component Identification.** We start with a concept that has been advocated in many multiview learning works. In particular, the views are nonlinear mixtures of shared and private latent components; see, e.g., (Huang et al., 2018; Lee et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2016). The shared components are distortion/view invariant and identity-revealing. The private components and view-specific nonlinear mixing processes determine the different appearances of the views. By assuming independence between the shared and private components and invertibility of the data generating process, we show that maximizing the correlation of latent representations extracted from different views leads to identification of the ground-truth shared components up to invertible transformations.

**(ii) Imposing Additional Constraints - Private Component Identification.** Using the understanding to latent correlation maximization-type loss functions in DCCA and AM-SSL, we take a step further. We show that with carefully imposed constraints, the private components in the views can also be identified, under reasonable assumptions. Learning private components can facilitate tasks such as cross-view and cross-sample data generation (Huang et al., 2018; Lee et al., 2018).

**(iii) Finite-Sample Analysis.** Most existing unsupervised nonlinear mixture identification works, e.g., those from the nonlinear independent component analysis (ICA) literature (Hyvarinen & Morioka, 2016; 2017; Hyvarinen et al., 2019; Khemakhem et al., 2020; Locatello et al., 2020; Gresele et al., 2020), are based on infinite data. This is perhaps because finite sample analysis for unsupervised learning is generally much more challenging relative to supervised cases—and there is no existing “universal” analytical tools. In this work, we provide sample complexity analysis for the proposed _unsupervised_ multiview learning criterion. We come up with a success metric for characterizing the performance of latent component extraction, and integrate generalization analysis and numerical differentiation to quantify this metric. To our best knowledge, this is the first finite-sample analysis of nonlinear mixture model-based multiview unsupervised learning.

**(iv) Practical Implementation.** Based on the theoretical understanding, we propose a latent correlation-maximization based multiview learning criterion for extracting both the shared components and private components. To realize the criterion, a notable innovation is a minimax neural regularizer that serves for extracting the private components. The regularizer shares the same purpose of some known independence promoters (e.g., _Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion_ (HSIC) (Gretton et al., 2007)) but is arguably easier to implement using stochastic gradient algorithms.

**Notation.** The notations used in this work are summarized in the supplementary material.

## 2.2 LATENT CORRELATION MAXIMIZATION/COMPONENT MATCHING IN AM-SSL

Similar to DCCA, the goal of AM-SSL is also to find identity-revealing embeddings of data samples without using labels. The idea is often realized via intentionally distorting the data to create multiple artificial views. Then, the encoders are require to produce highly correlated (or closely matched) embeddings from such views. In AM-SSL, the views _**x**_[(1)] and _**x**_[(2)] are different augmentations (e.g., by adding noise, cropping, and rotation) of the sample _**x**_ .

_•_ **Barlow Twins.** The most recent development, namely, the Barlow Twins network (Zbontar et al., 2021) is appealing since it entails a succinct implementation. ## 3 UNDERSTANDING LATENT CORRELATION MAXIMIZATION

In this section, we offer understandings to latent correlation maximization (and latent component matching) from an unsupervised nonlinear multiview mixture identification viewpoint. We will also show that such understanding can help improve multiview learning criteria to serve different purposes, e.g., cross-view and cross-sample data generation.

Assumption 1 is considered reasonable under both AM-SSL and DCCA settings. For AM-SSL, the private information can be understood as random data augmentation noise-induced components, and thus it makes sense to assume that such noise is independent with the shared information (which corresponds to the identity-revealing components of the data sample). In DCCA problems, the private style information can change drastically from view to view (e.g., audio, text, video) without changing the shared content information (e.g., identity of the entity)—which also shows independence between the two parts. In our analysis, we will assume that _D_ and _Dq_ are known to facilitate exposition. In practice, these parameters are often selected using a validation set.

**Learning Goals.** Our interest lies in extracting _**z** ℓ_ and _**c**_[(] _ℓ[q]_[)] (up to certain ambiguities) from the views in an unsupervised manner. In particular, we hope to answer under what conditions these latent components can be identified—and to what extent. As mentioned, _**z** ℓ_ is view/distortioninvariant and thus should be identity-revealing. The ability of extracting it may explain DCCA and AM-SSL’s effectiveness. In addition, the identification of _**c**_[(] _ℓ[q]_[)] and the mixing processes may help generate data in different views.

## 4 IMPLEMENTATION

**Enforcing Group Statistical Independence.** A notable challenge is the statistical independence constraint in (7d), whose enforcement is often an art. Early methods such as (Taleb & Jutten, 1999; Hyv¨arinen & Oja, 2000) may be costly. The HSIC method in (Gretton et al., 2007) which measures the correlation of two variables in a kernel space can be used in our framework, but kernels sometimes induce large memory overheads and are sensitive to parameter (e.g., kernel width) selection.

## 5 RELATED WORK - NONLINEAR ICA AND LATENT DISENTANGLEMENT

Other than the DCCA and AM-SSL works, our design for private and shared information separation also draws insights from two topics in unsupervised representation learning, namely, nonlinear ICA (nICA) (Hyvarinen & Morioka, 2016; 2017; Hyvarinen et al., 2019; Khemakhem et al., 2020) and latent factor disentanglement (Higgins et al., 2017; Kim & Mnih, 2018; Chen et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2019; Lopez et al., 2018), which are recently offered a unifying perspective in (Khemakhem et al., 2020). The nICA works aim to _separate_ nonlinearly mixed latent components to an individual component level, which is in general impossible unless additional information associated with each sample (e.g., time frame labels (Hyvarinen & Morioka, 2016) and class labels (Hyvarinen et al., 2019; Khemakhem et al., 2020)) is used. Multiple views are less studied in the context of nICA, with the recent exception in (Locatello et al., 2020) and (Gresele et al., 2020). Nonetheless, their models are different from ours and the approaches cannot extract the private information from views with different nonlinear models. ## 6.1 VALIDATING THEOREM 1 - SHARED COMPONENT LEARNING

Using this multiview data, we apply different multiview learning paradigms that match the latent representations (or maximize the correlations of learned representations) across views. ## 7 CONCLUSION

In this work, we provided theoretical understandings to the role of latent correlation maximization (latent component matching) that is often used in DCCA and AM-SSL methods from an unsupervised nonlinear mixture learning viewpoint. In particular, we modeled multiview data as nonlinear mixtures of shared and private components, and showed that latent correlation maximization ensures to extract the shared components—which are believed to be identity-revealing. In addition, we showed that, with a carefully designed constraint (which is approximated by a neural regularizer), one can further disentangle the shared and private information, under reasonable conditions. We also analyzed the sample complexity for extracting the shared information, which has not been addressed in nonlinear component analysis works, to our best knowledge. To realize our learning criterion, we proposed a slack variable-assisted latent correlation maximization approach, with a novel minimax neural regularizer for promoting group independence. We tested our method over synthetic and real data. The results corroborated our design goals and theoretical analyses.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper is characterized by a high degree of technical rigor and provides a timely theoretical bridge between empirical successes in deep multiview learning and the mathematical foundations of latent component identification. The authors successfully elevate the discourse from mere intuition to a formal generative framework, effectively situating canonical correlation analysis (CCA) and modern self-supervised learning (SSL) paradigms within the broader field of nonlinear independent component analysis. The most significant strength of the work is its ambition: by addressing the "black box" nature of correlated representation learning through rigorous identifiability guarantees and, critically, providing a novel finite-sample complexity analysis, the study addresses a notable gap in the literature. However, the complexity of the proposed framework poses a potential challenge regarding accessibility; the transition from abstract identifiability theory to the practical implementation of the "minimax neural regularizer" will require careful exposition to ensure that the theoretical insights translate clearly into actionable guidance for readers. Overall, the paper demonstrates high potential to impact the field by providing a disciplined, theory-backed structure for designing more robust and interpretable multiview self-supervised learning architectures.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: This paper tackles an important theoretical gap in multiview and self-supervised learning by providing an identifiability analysis for latent correlation maximization, and its strengths lie in the clear motivation, structured contributions (shared/private component identification, finite-sample analysis), and the proposed minimax regularizer. The quality of the exposition is high, with a well-organized introduction that effectively contextualizes the work within existing literature. However, a significant issue is the reliance on a specific generative model (nonlinear mixtures of independent shared and private components with invertible mixing); this assumption may be too restrictive for many real-world multiview settings (e.g., where views are not fully invertible or where shared/private independence is violated), and the paper could better discuss the practical implications of such violations. Additionally, the finite-sample analysis, while novel, appears to depend on smoothness and invertibility conditions that are difficult to verify in practice, and the connection to empirical SSL methods (e.g., BYOL, Barlow Twins) is asserted but not deeply examined in terms of how their specific loss functions align with the proposed theoretical framework. Overall, the paper is a valuable theoretical contribution, but its impact would be strengthened by a more thorough discussion of these limitations and a clearer mapping between theory and the practical algorithms it claims to explain.

However, Theorem 3 in the summary gives a bound involving κ_j = Ω((3/C_d)^{1/3} (4C_f(2D R_N + C_f √(log(1/δ)/2N) + 4ν^2)^{1/6}). With R_N = O(N^{-1/2}) (as mentioned in the passage), the dominant term inside the parentheses is O(N^{-1/2}), and raising to the 1/6 power gives O(N^{-1/12}). Even if the 1/3 factor outside is considered, the overall rate appears to be O(N^{-1/12}) rather than O(N^{-1/3}). Without seeing the exact metric definition and the full bound from the original paper, this claim seems inconsistent with the theorem as presented. A convergence rate of N^{-1/3} is substantially faster than N^{-1/12}, so the passage may mislead readers about the strength of the finite-sample guarantee.

comments.title: Overextension of Theorem 1 to benchmark methods
comments.quote: By Theorem 1, all the methods under test should output identity-revealing representations of the data samples.
comments.explanation: Theorem 1 is proved for the specific optimization criterion (7) with explicit constraints (invertibility, orthogonality, zero-mean). The benchmark methods (DCCA, Barlow Twins, BYOL) do not enforce all these constraints—in particular, invertibility is not guaranteed. Claiming that Theorem 1 directly implies these methods output identity-revealing representations overstates the theoretical coverage. While the empirical results are consistent, the theoretical justification is insufficient for methods that do not solve (7). This could mislead readers into thinking the theorem applies more broadly than proven.

comments.title: Ambiguity in modeling azimuth as private component
comments.quote: We assume that given the car type that is captured by shared variables z and the azimuths that are captured by c^{(q)}, the generation mappings g^{(1)} and g^{(2)} produce car images with low elevations and high elevations, respectively (so they must be different mappings).
comments.explanation: In the generative model (5), private components c^{(q)} are view-specific. Azimuth is a property of the car that could plausibly be the same across both views if the same car is imaged at the same angle. If azimuth is shared across views, it should be part of the shared component z, not the private component. The text does not clarify whether azimuth is view-specific or shared; the experiment treats it as private within each view for cross-sample generation. This creates ambiguity about whether the experimental setup adheres to the generative model assumed in Theorem 2. The successful disentanglement is not undermined, but the modeling choice deserves clarification.

progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive Original (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: This paper tackles an important theoretical gap in multiview and self-supervised learning by providing an identifiability analysis for latent correlation maximization, and its strengths lie in the clear motivation, structured contributions (shared/private component identification, finite-sample analysis), and the proposed minimax regularizer. The quality of the exposition is high, with a well-organized introduction that effectively contextualizes the work within existing literature. However, a significant issue is the reliance on a specific generative model (nonlinear mixtures of independent shared and private components with invertible mixing); this assumption may be too restrictive for many real-world multiview settings (e.g., where views are not fully invertible or where shared/private independence is violated), and the paper could better discuss the practical implications of such violations. Additionally, the finite-sample analysis, while novel, appears to depend on smoothness and invertibility conditions that are difficult to verify in practice, and the connection to empirical SSL methods (e.g., BYOL, Barlow Twins) is asserted but not deeply examined in terms of how their specific loss functions align with the proposed theoretical framework. Overall, the paper is a valuable theoretical contribution, but its impact would be strengthened by a more thorough discussion of these limitations and a clearer mapping between theory and the practical algorithms it claims to explain.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper tackles a timely and conceptually sound problem by offering an identifiability-based theoretical foundation for correlation-driven multiview and self-supervised learning, effectively unifying DCCA and AM-SSL under a nonlinear mixture generative model. The motivation is clearly articulated, the writing is generally precise, and the ambition to deliver finite-sample guarantees for unsupervised nonlinear mixture learning is commendable. However, several high-level issues require careful attention: the identifiability claims closely parallel well-established results in nonlinear ICA and contrastive representation learning, so the novelty of the analytical framework and the restrictiveness of its core assumptions (e.g., strict independence, invertibility, and specific regularizer forms) must be explicitly delineated from prior work. Additionally, while the finite-sample analysis is promising, bridging population-risk identifiability to practical guarantees for over-parameterized, non-convex neural optimizers remains a nontrivial theoretical hurdle that is not yet convincingly addressed. Finally, the transition from theoretical constraints to the proposed “minimax neural regularizer” appears underdeveloped; clarity is needed on how the regularizer concretely operationalizes the identifiability conditions without introducing optimization instability or violating the stated assumptions. Overall, the manuscript is well-structured and conceptually strong, but its theoretical contributions will be significantly strengthened by sharper differentiation from existing identifiability literature, more realistic modeling of deep learning optimization dynamics, and a clearer exposition of how the proposed regularizer enforces the theoretical guarantees in practice.

comments.title: Ambiguity regarding the role of "numerical differentiation" in the theoretical contribution
comments.quote: We come up with a success metric for characterizing the performance of latent component extraction, and integrate generalization analysis and numerical differentiation to quantify this metric.
comments.explanation: The authors introduce this phrase in contribution (iii), which is explicitly framed as a theoretical "Finite-Sample Analysis". While generalization analysis (e.g., using Rademacher complexity or covering numbers) is standard for deriving analytical sample complexity bounds, "numerical differentiation" is typically a computational technique used in experimental settings to approximate gradients or evaluate metrics on finite datasets, rather than a tool for theoretical proofs. A careful reader might initially expect this to describe a step in the mathematical derivation of the finite-sample bound, which could be misleading. The authors likely refer to using numerical differentiation in their empirical evaluation to compute the proposed success metric on finite samples, but mentioning it here without clarifying whether it applies to the theoretical framework or the experimental protocol creates minor ambiguity. Clarifying that numerical differentiation is reserved for empirical quantification would resolve this.

comments.title: Notation Overloading for Dq (Distribution vs. Dimension)
comments.quote: where _**x**_[(] _[q]_[)] _∈_ R _[M][q] ∼Dq_ is a data sample from view _q_ for _q_ = 1 _,_ 2, _Dq_ is the underlying distribution of the _q_ th view
comments.explanation: Initially, I noted that Dq is introduced to denote the probability distribution of view q. Later in Section 3.1, the text states: 'we will assume that D and Dq are known to facilitate exposition. In practice, these parameters are often selected using a validation set.' In representation learning, D and Dq clearly refer to the dimensions of the shared and private latent spaces, respectively, which are hyperparameters tuned on validation data. Distributions, however, are empirical properties of the data and cannot be 'selected'. The authors likely overloaded Dq for brevity, but this creates a logical ambiguity: readers may mistakenly believe Dq is a tunable distribution parameter rather than a latent dimension. Disentangling these into distinct symbols (e.g., P_q for distribution, d_q for dimension) would resolve the confusion.

comments.title: Incomplete Sentence Referencing Missing Formal Criterion
comments.quote: To denote the encoder-extracted shared and private components for each view, respectively. Note that designating the first _D_ dimensions of the encoder outputs to represent the shared information is without loss of generality, since the permutation ambiguity is intrinsic.
comments.explanation: This sentence fragment immediately precedes Theorem 1 but lacks a grammatical subject and mathematical definition, almost certainly due to the loss of equation (7) during PDF extraction. The authors intend to formally define the latent correlation-based learning criterion and justify the architectural choice of splitting the first D dimensions for shared components. However, without the explicit equation, it is difficult for a careful reader to verify the precise constraints (zero-mean, orthogonality, statistical independence) referenced in Theorems 1-3. While I understand the intent, the missing formal statement creates a reproducibility gap. Providing the complete criterion or a fully spelled-out textual definition would fix this.

comments.title: Corrupted Symbol in Sample Complexity Bound (Theorem 3)
comments.explanation: This expression defining the sample complexity bound contains several symbols that are mathematically inconsistent or appear corrupted. `[3]` is likely `3`, `Cd` and `Cf` should be `C_d` and `C_f`, and the `‖` preceding `log(1/δ) / 2 N` is almost certainly a misread `√` (square root), which is standard in concentration inequalities (yielding `√(log(1/δ)/(2N))`). Additionally, `ν_2` likely denotes `ν^2`. Assuming these plausible corrections reveals a meaningful bias-variance-like tradeoff between expressiveness and sample requirements, but the current notation obscures the exact dependence on the confidence parameter δ and the norm used in the finite-sample analysis. Correcting these to standard notation would ensure the theoretical contribution is properly interpreted and reproducible.

comments.title: Ambiguous Normalization Notation in BYOL Description
comments.quote: where _**f**_ ~~(~~ _q_ )( _·_ ) means that the output of the network is normalized.
comments.explanation: The notation `~~(~~` is a clear OCR artifact or typesetting glitch. In the context of BYOL, the authors intend to describe L2-normalization (e.g., `‖f^(q)(·)‖ = 1` or a normalized output vector). However, the exact mathematical form is unreadable. Without clarifying whether normalization applies to the predictor network, the target network, or the encoder output, readers cannot precisely replicate the cross-view matching mechanism described. While the conceptual intent is clear, the missing notation is a practical barrier for implementation.

comments.title: Missing square root in Theorem 3 bound contradicts stated sample complexity
comments.explanation: The OCR artifact '' almost certainly represents a missing square root symbol (\\sqrt{\\cdot}), which is standard in generalization bounds involving logarithmic confidence terms. As written, the term inside the parentheses scales as O(1/N). Taking the 1/6-th root would yield a scaling of O(N^{-1/6}). However, the authors immediately state that 'the metric is bounded by O(N^{-1/3})'. This claimed rate is only mathematically consistent if the inner generalization term scales as O(N^{-1/2}) (i.e., if the square root was intended). Without it, the formula directly contradicts the authors' stated sample complexity result.

comments.title: Unexplained index in Theorem 3 condition
comments.quote: for any _**c**_[(] _[q]_[)] _∈Cq such that −Cp_ + _κj ≤ c_[(] _j[q]_[)] _≤ Cp − _κj for all j ∈_ [ _Dq_ ] _and all i ∈_ [ _D_ ] _, where κj_ = Ω(...)
comments.explanation: The theorem bounds the private latent components c_j^{[q]} for indices j ∈ [D_q]. The additional clause 'and all i ∈ [D]' introduces a completely undefined index i that does not appear in the inequality, the definition of κ_j, or the surrounding context. This is likely a typographical error (e.g., inadvertently repeating a range or confusing sample index i with dimension index), creating ambiguity about the exact quantification of the bound.

comments.title: Garbled notation in Assumption 2(c)
comments.explanation: The notation 'G[−]_[1]' is non-standard and likely a corruption of a proper class name (e.g., G_{inv} or G_{-1} intended to denote inverse mappings). Furthermore, '[D]_1' appears to be a corrupted form of 'D_1', especially given the preceding sentence assumes D_1 = D_2 = D for simplicity. These errors obscure the precise definition of the approximation class and its dimensionality, requiring the reader to guess the intended symbols.

comments.title: Notation abuse in Theorems 1 and 2 (variable vs function output)
comments.explanation: The theorems equate the random latent variables (z and c^{[q]}) directly with the deterministic outputs of the encoder functions, and use 'x' as the argument instead of the view-specific 'x^{[q]}' defined in the generative model. While this shorthand is common in representation learning literature, it technically conflates random variables with function evaluations and drops the view subscript. A careful reader might initially question whether 'x' refers to a generic input or specifically x^{[q]}, though context resolves this quickly.

comments.title: Overstatement of Theorem 1's direct applicability to baseline methods
comments.quote: By Theorem 1, all the methods under test should output identity-revealing representations of the data samples. The reason is that the two views share the same identity information of a sample.
comments.explanation: The authors state that Theorem 1 guarantees identity-revealing outputs for the baseline methods (DCCA, Barlow Twins, BYOL). However, Theorem 1 is proven specifically for the authors' constrained optimization formulation (Eq. 7), which explicitly enforces group statistical independence (constraint 7d), invertibility, and zero-mean/orthogonality constraints. The benchmark methods do not strictly match these exact constraints or the joint shared/private factorization assumed in the theorem. While it is scientifically reasonable to hypothesize that latent correlation maximization plays a unifying role across these methods, claiming that Theorem 1 directly applies to them is a logical leap. The theorem rigorously supports the proposed method, but its extension to baselines remains empirical intuition rather than a proven guarantee. This slightly overstates the theoretical foundation for the baseline comparisons.

comments.title: Ambiguity regarding latent variable assignment for 'elevation' in Cars3D
comments.quote: We assume that given the car type that is captured by shared variables **z** and the azimuths that are captured by **c**[(] _[q]_[)] , the generation mappings **g**[(1)] and **g**[(2)] produce car images with low elevations and high elevations, respectively (so they must be different mappings)... this generated sample **x** ... should exhibit the same ‘type’ and ‘elevation’ as those of **x**[(] _ℓ[q]_[)]
comments.explanation: The text explicitly assigns 'type' to the shared latent **z** and 'azimuth' to the private latent **c**^(q), but does not explicitly state where the 'elevation' attribute resides. It is implied that elevation is a view-specific deterministic property fixed by the choice of **g**^(1) vs **g**^(2). However, the subsequent claim that generated samples should have the 'same ‘type’ and ‘elevation’ as **x**^(ℓ(q))' could reasonably mislead a careful reader into thinking elevation is either captured by a latent variable or varies across samples within a view. Clarifying that elevation is entirely deterministic per view and handled by the view-specific reconstruction network **r**^(q) would resolve this ambiguity and align with the identifiability framework presented.

comments.title: OCR corruption and notation inconsistency in cross-sample generation formula
comments.explanation: The mathematical expression for the cross-sample generated sample is heavily corrupted by OCR artifacts (e.g., misplaced hats, broken brackets, and phantom characters like ``). The intended formula is almost certainly $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{\ell,j}^{(q)} = \mathbf{r}^{(q)}([(\mathbf{z}_{\ell}^{(q)})^\top, ((\mathbf{c}_{j}^{(q)})^\top]^\top)$. While the surrounding prose correctly describes the procedure of concatenating shared and private components, the garbled notation violates standard mathematical formatting, breaks subscript/superscript consistency with the rest of the paper, and hinders precise reproducibility. Correcting this to match the paper's established notation conventions is necessary.

The additional clause 'and all i ∈ [D]' introduces a completely undefined index i that does not appear in the inequality, the definition of κ_j, or the surrounding context. This is likely a typographical error (e.g., inadvertently repeating a range or confusing sample index i with dimension index), creating ambiguity about the exact quantification of the bound.

comments.title: Garbled notation in Assumption 2(c)
comments.explanation: The notation 'G[−]_[1]' is non-standard and likely a corruption of a proper class name (e.g., G_{inv} or G_{-1} intended to denote inverse mappings). Furthermore, '[D]_1' appears to be a corrupted form of 'D_1', especially given the preceding sentence assumes D_1 = D_2 = D for simplicity. These errors obscure the precise definition of the approximation class and its dimensionality, requiring the reader to guess the intended symbols.

comments.title: Notation abuse in Theorems 1 and 2 (variable vs function output)
comments.explanation: The theorems equate the random latent variables (z and c^{[q]}) directly with the deterministic outputs of the encoder functions, and use 'x' as the argument instead of the view-specific 'x^{[q]}' defined in the generative model. While this shorthand is common in representation learning literature, it technically conflates random variables with function evaluations and drops the view subscript. A careful reader might initially question whether 'x' refers to a generic input or specifically x^{[q]}, though context resolves this quickly.

comments.title: Overstatement of Theorem 1's direct applicability to baseline methods
comments.quote: By Theorem 1, all the methods under test should output identity-revealing representations of the data samples. The reason is that the two views share the same identity information of a sample.
comments.explanation: The authors state that Theorem 1 guarantees identity-revealing outputs for the baseline methods (DCCA, Barlow Twins, BYOL). However, Theorem 1 is proven specifically for the authors' constrained optimization formulation (Eq. 7), which explicitly enforces group statistical independence (constraint 7d), invertibility, and zero-mean/orthogonality constraints. The benchmark methods do not strictly match these exact constraints or the joint shared/private factorization assumed in the theorem. While it is scientifically reasonable to hypothesize that latent correlation maximization plays a unifying role across these methods, claiming that Theorem 1 directly applies to them is a logical leap. The theorem rigorously supports the proposed method, but its extension to baselines remains empirical intuition rather than a proven guarantee. This slightly overstates the theoretical foundation for the baseline comparisons.

comments.title: Ambiguity regarding latent variable assignment for 'elevation' in Cars3D
comments.quote: We assume that given the car type that is captured by shared variables **z** and the azimuths that are captured by **c**[(] _[q]_[)] , the generation mappings **g**[(1)] and **g**[(2)] produce car images with low elevations and high elevations, respectively (so they must be different mappings)... this generated sample **x** ... should exhibit the same ‘type’ and ‘elevation’ as those of **x**[(] _ℓ[q]_[)]
comments.explanation: The text explicitly assigns 'type' to the shared latent **z** and 'azimuth' to the private latent **c**^(q), but does not explicitly state where the 'elevation' attribute resides. It is implied that elevation is a view-specific deterministic property fixed by the choice of **g**^(1) vs **g**^(2). However, the subsequent claim that generated samples should have the 'same ‘type’ and ‘elevation’ as **x**^(ℓ(q))' could reasonably mislead a careful reader into thinking elevation is either captured by a latent variable or varies across samples within a view. Clarifying that elevation is entirely deterministic per view and handled by the view-specific reconstruction network **r**^(q) would resolve this ambiguity and align with the identifiability framework presented.

comments.title: OCR corruption and notation inconsistency in cross-sample generation formula
comments.explanation: The mathematical expression for the cross-sample generated sample is heavily corrupted by OCR artifacts (e.g., misplaced hats, broken brackets, and phantom characters like ``). The intended formula is almost certainly $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{\ell,j}^{(q)} = \mathbf{r}^{(q)}([(\mathbf{z}_{\ell}^{(q)})^\top, ((\mathbf{c}_{j}^{(q)})^\top]^\top)$. While the surrounding prose correctly describes the procedure of concatenating shared and private components, the garbled notation violates standard mathematical formatting, breaks subscript/superscript consistency with the rest of the paper, and hinders precise reproducibility. Correcting this to match the paper's established notation conventions is necessary.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a valuable theoretical perspective on the role of latent correlation maximization in deep multiview learning and self-supervised learning, effectively bridging the gap between algorithmic practices (e.g., DCCA, BYOL) and the nonlinear mixture model perspective. The authors make a strong case that maximizing cross-view correlations induces identifiability of shared components, a finding that offers significant explanatory power for empirical successes in the field. A particularly notable contribution is the provision of finite-sample analysis, which addresses a significant gap in the nonlinear mixture identifiability literature and justifies the practical performance of the proposed methods. However, clarity could be improved regarding the precise conditions under which the introduced minimax regularizer stabilizes the optimization landscape, and the abrupt cutoff in the text ("shares the s") disrupts the flow of the contribution list.

comments.title: Confusing Causality in Sample Complexity Trade-off Explanation
comments.quote: When $R_N$ increases (e.g., by using a more complex neural network), the function mismatch $\nu$ often decreases (since $F$ can be more expressive with a higher $R_N$).
comments.explanation: The sentence attempts to explain the trade-off between function expressiveness and sample complexity. However, the parenthetical clause is logically inverted. Increasing the complexity of the neural network ($F$) causes $R_N$ (the Rademacher complexity bound) to increase, and also reduces the mismatch $\nu$. The text implies that $R_N$ somehow confers expressivity upon $F$ ('more expressive with a higher $R_N$'), whereas $R_N$ is a measure of class complexity and is increased by expressiveness, not the other way around. This ambiguity makes the reasoning difficult to follow.

comments.title: Potentially Incorrect Formula Representation (OCR Artifact)
comments.quote: An equivalent representation of (1) is as follows ... $\sum_{i,j=1}^{D} \mathbf{f}_i^T(\mathbf{x}_1) \mathbf{f}_j^T(\mathbf{x}_2) = ...$
comments.explanation: The provided text depicts a double sum over indices $i$ and $j$ of the inner products of projections. The standard Deep CCA (DCCA) objective typically maximizes the trace of the cross-covariance matrix, which corresponds to a single sum over $i$ (i.e., $\sum_{i=1}^D$). A double sum over $i$ and $j$ implies summing cross-correlations between different components ($f_1$ with $f_2$, $f_2$ with $f_1$, etc.), which is not the standard DCCA formulation. While this is likely an OCR reconstruction artifact, it presents an incorrect mathematical representation in the text.

comments.title: Inconsistent Notation for Shared Component Indexing
comments.quote: where $\mathbf{x}_{(\ell^{(q)})} \in \mathbb{R}^{M^{(q)}}$ is the $\ell$ th sample... and $\mathbf{z}_\ell \in \mathbb{R}^{D}$ is the shared component across views...
comments.explanation: The notation for the shared component $\mathbf{z}$ consistently uses a subscript $\ell$ (as in $\mathbf{z}_\ell$) throughout the definitions and theorems. However, in the generative model description, the shared component is defined as $\mathbf{z} \in \mathbb{R}^{D}$ without the subscript $\ell$, appearing in the argument of the mixing function as $\mathbf{g}^{(q)}(\mathbf{z}^	op, ...)$. This slight inconsistency in explicitly tying the shared component to the sample index $\ell$ in the definition makes the flow slightly less rigorous than it could be.

comments.title: Discrepancy between text claim and mathematical bound scaling
comments.quote: The theorem indicates that with N samples, the metric is bounded by O(N[−]_[1] _[/]_[3] )
comments.explanation: The passage discusses Theorem 3 (Sample Complexity) and explicitly states that the resulting metric is bounded by O(N^{-1/3}). However, the Summary section provided defines the error bound \kappa_j as \mathcal{O}( ( ... / N )^{1/6} ), which implies a scaling of O(N^{-1/6}). These two claims are mathematically inconsistent. Given the typical results for nonlinear estimation error bounds (e.g., analogous to ICA rates), O(N^{-1/6}) is plausible, while O(N^{-1/3}) represents a significant deviation. If this is not a typo in the passage text, it contradicts the formal derivation presented in the Summary. Even considering the potential OCR nature of the text, the inconsistency undermines confidence in the specific rate claimed in the prose.

comments.title: Missing equation in reformulation description
comments.quote: The overall formulation is:
comments.explanation: In the 'Reformulation and Optimization' section, the authors introduce Equation (10) as 'the overall formulation'. However, the equation itself is not visible in the passage; instead, the text immediately follows with the definitions of the constituent variables (L, V, U, R) without showing the summation or combination that constitutes the main optimization problem. This omission is problematic for readers attempting to verify the proposed method, as it obscures how the independence regularization term (derived in the previous paragraph) is integrated into the loss function.

comments.title: Incorrect publication year for Chen et al. (2018)
comments.quote: Ricky TQ Chen, Xuechen Li, Roger B Grosse, and David K Duvenaud. Isolating sources of disentanglement in variational autoencoders. In Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, pp. 2610–2620, 2018.
comments.explanation: The paper "Isolating sources of disentanglement in variational autoencoders" by Ricky TQ Chen et al. was submitted to and accepted by NeurIPS in 2018, but was published in 2019 (as Volume 32 of the proceedings). While the submission year is 2018, the publication year listed in standard academic citations must be 2019. The reference incorrectly lists 2018 as the publication year. This is a factual error in the bibliographic entry, not an OCR reading error of the year itself, as '2018' is explicitly stated at the end of the entry.
